subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
title: RBI Monetary Policy: Quantitative and Qualitative Tools
focus: Understanding the instruments used by the RBI to control money supply and direct credit flow.
rationale: This is a foundational economic concept covering the core tools of monetary policy, which is highly examinable in UPSC prelims.
sectionExcerpt: QUANTITATIVE TOOLS (Volume of Credit) - Directly impact the overall money supply: REPO RATE (Lending Rate): Rate at which RBI lends short-term to banks. Main signal rate. REVERSE REPO RATE (Borrowing Rate): Rate at which RBI borrows from banks. CRR (Cash Reserve Ratio): % of net demand & time liabilities (NDTL) banks must keep as CASH with RBI. No interest paid. SLR (Statutory Liquidity Ratio): % of NDTL banks must maintain in LIQUID ASSETS. QUALITATIVE TOOLS (Direction of Credit) - Selectively influence credit flow to specific sectors: MORAL SUASION... MARGIN REQUIREMENTS... DIRECT ACTION...
evidenceBullets: Repo Rate is the rate at which RBI lends short-term to banks; increasing it decreases money supply.
CRR is the percentage of NDTL banks must keep as cash with RBI, earning no interest.
SLR is the percentage of NDTL banks must maintain in liquid assets like gold, bonds, or cash.
Qualitative tools include Moral Suasion, Margin Requirements (changing loan-to-value ratio), and Direct Action.

subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
title: RBI Surplus Transfer and Economic Capital Framework (ECF)
focus: The mechanism, framework, and components involved in the transfer of RBI's surplus to the Central Government.
rationale: The ECF and surplus transfer are critical aspects of RBI-Government relations and fiscal management, frequently tested in the exam.
sectionExcerpt: RBI has transferred Rs 2.69 Lakh Crore surplus to centre. The transferable surplus for 2024-25 has been arrived at on the basis of the revised Economic Capital Framework (ECF)... Genesis: ECF was adopted by RBI in 2019, based on the recommendations of the Bimal Jalan Committee. Components of Economic Capital under ECF: Realized Equity... Contingent Risk Buffer (CRB): Component of RBI's realized equity to provide for monetary and financial stability... Revaluation Balances...
evidenceBullets: RBI transferred Rs 2.69 Lakh Crore surplus to the centre for 2024-25 based on the Economic Capital Framework (ECF).
The ECF was adopted in 2019 based on the recommendations of the Bimal Jalan Committee.
RBI's income sources include interest on government securities, liquidity management tools, seigniorage, and foreign currency investments.
Economic Capital components under ECF include Realized Equity, Contingent Risk Buffer (CRB), and Revaluation Balances.

subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
title: India's Forex Reserves and Gold Holdings
focus: Composition of India's foreign exchange reserves and the rationale and risks associated with increasing gold reserves.
rationale: Forex reserves composition is a classic UPSC topic, and the recent surge in gold reserves adds current relevance.
sectionExcerpt: According to the Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) latest foreign exchange reserves data, gold reserves have reached to $102.3 billion. This has taken the share of the gold in the foreign reserves to nearly 15%... Why is RBI increasing gold reserves? Diversification... Risk Mitigation... Hedge against Inflation... Risk Associated with Storing Gold: Reduced Liquidity and Flexibility... Zero Yield... Components of Foreign Exchange Reserves: Foreign Currency Assets (FCA)... Gold Reserves... Holdings with IMF... Special Drawing Rights... Reserve Tranche Position (RTP)...
evidenceBullets: Gold reserves constitute nearly 15% of India's foreign reserves, reaching $102.3 billion.
RBI increases gold reserves for diversification (de-dollarization), risk mitigation, and hedging against inflation.
Risks of storing gold include reduced liquidity, zero yield, and high storage and security costs.
Forex reserves consist of Foreign Currency Assets (FCA), Gold, Special Drawing Rights (SDR), and Reserve Tranche Position (RTP).

subtopic: RBI Monetary Policy and Surplus Management
title: Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021
focus: Expansion of the RB-IOS to include Co-operative Banks and its core objectives.
rationale: Regulatory changes and grievance redressal mechanisms in banking are important for polity and economy sections.
sectionExcerpt: The Central and State Co-operative Banks have been brought under the ambit of Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021 under Banking regulation act, 1949. About Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021 (RB-IOS, 2021) - Objective: Provide customers of regulated entities (REs) a speedy, cost-effective and expeditious alternate grievance redress mechanism.
evidenceBullets: Central and State Co-operative Banks are now covered under the Reserve Bank - Integrated Ombudsman Scheme, 2021.
The inclusion of Co-operative Banks was done under the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
The scheme aims to provide a speedy and cost-effective alternate grievance redress mechanism for customers of regulated entities.
